coredata.dc:title: “Ball-rod” shaped multifunctional magnetic Janus nanoparticles for efficient drug delivery
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

coredata.dc:creator: He, Yuhai
Teng, Yi
Wu, Qiuhua
Zhang, Guolin
coredata.dc:description: Magnetic Janus nanoparticles (MJNPs) have attracted extensive attention in the biomedical field due to their asymmetrical composition and independent functional partitioning. In this paper, “ball-rod” shaped drug delivery systems (DDSs) with CoFe2O4 balls and mesoporous silica (mSiO2) rods, which were coated with CDs via imine bonds, were prepared. The DDSs were named CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs MJNPs and DOX was used as a model drug to form DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2. Capping agent CDs blocked the pore entrance and endowed the carriers with negatively charged surface. CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs showed fine magnetic properties and microwave (MW) thermal transition properties contributed to the ball portion of CoFe2O4. The drug release behavior of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs showed pH/MW dual-responsiveness. The broken of imine bonds resulted in the detachment of CDs and the NPs became positively charged. The new NPs provided new insights for designing multi-functional DDSs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Janus
Mesoporous silica
Dual-sensitive
Magnetic targeting
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability and responsiveness of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs MJNPs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Jinzhou Science and Technology Program (JZ2023B068) and the Transverse Project of Jinzhou Medical University (H2023033/H2023039).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow diagram of preparation and DOX release of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs.

Preparation and characterization of CDs. (a) Preparation process. (b) TEM image (inset: enlarged image). (c) Particle size distribution graph. (d) XRD pattern. (e) FT-IR spectrum. (f) PL intensity at various excitation wavelengths. (g) Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity and (h) UV-Vis spectrum.

(a) XRD pattern. (b) FT-IR spectra of CoFe2O4 (1), CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB (2), CoFe2O4&mSiO2-NH2 (3), CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO (4), CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (5). (c) EDX curves of different NPs. (d) The Zeta potential of CoFe2O4 (1), CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB (2), CoFe2O4&mSiO2-NH2 (3), CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO (4), CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (5), DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (6), CDs (7) and DOX (8) in PBS at pH=7.4. (e) TGA patterns. (f) GA grafting mechanism diagram.
(a) SEM and (b) TEM (inset: DLS) images of CoFe2O4 NPs. (c, d, and e) SEM, (f) TEM of CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB NPs. (g) SEM and (h, i) TEM of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs NPs.

(a) Hysteresis loops curves (inset: magnetic separation images). (b) Low-field magnification image. (c) MW thermal conversion heating curves of various NPs. (d) MW thermal conversion heating curves of various concentrations of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs. (e) The Zeta potential of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs. (f) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of CoFe2O4&mSiO2 and DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2 (inset: pore size distribution of adsorption branches).
Cumulative release percentage curves of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (a) at 37 ℃, different pH. (b) At pH=6.2, different temperatures. (c) At 37 ℃, different pH under MW irradiation.

Inverted fluorescence microscopy images of endocytosis in MCF-7 cells with DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs; Red emission from DOX was collected at 560−600 nm under 480−490 nm excitation; Green fluorescence emission was collected at 500−550 nm through 400−450 nm excitation.

(a) Cell viability after co-incubation with different doses of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs. Cell viability of (b) Hela, (c) A549, and (d) MCF-7 co-incubation with different doses of free DOX, DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs, and DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs under MW irradiation.

head.ce:title: “Ball-rod” shaped multifunctional magnetic Janus nanoparticles for efficient drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Medicine, Jinzhou Medical University, Jinzhou 121001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Basic Medicine, Jinzhou Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Basic Medicine, Jinzhou Medical University, Jinzhou 121001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnetic Janus nanoparticles (MJNPs) have attracted extensive attention in the biomedical field due to their asymmetrical composition and independent functional partitioning. In this paper, “ball-rod” shaped drug delivery systems (DDSs) with CoFe2O4 balls and mesoporous silica (mSiO2) rods, which were coated with CDs via imine bonds, were prepared. The DDSs were named CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs MJNPs and DOX was used as a model drug to form DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2. Capping agent CDs blocked the pore entrance and endowed the carriers with negatively charged surface. CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs showed fine magnetic properties and microwave (MW) thermal transition properties contributed to the ball portion of CoFe2O4. The drug release behavior of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs showed pH/MW dual-responsiveness. The broken of imine bonds resulted in the detachment of CDs and the NPs became positively charged. The new NPs provided new insights for designing multi-functional DDSs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy has been widely accepted as one of the most effective means of treating cancer. Anticancer drugs such as Doxorubicin (DOX) could effectively inhibit the growth of tumor cells, while normal tissue also subject to serious damage. Drug delivery systems (DDSs) with various functions such as targeting, controlled release, multi-drug delivery, image guidance, and diagnosis, have been developed to relieve the side effects of drugs. Mesopores SiO2 (mSiO2) is one of the widely used DDSs because of its merits of good stability, better biocompatibility, easily adjustable pore structure, and ease of surface modification [1]. However, lacking intelligent release and targeting properties restrict the application of mSiO2 [2,3]. The negatively charged surface attributed to rich silicon hydroxyl groups results in poor affinity to the cell membrane. Surface modifications and capping strategies are effective ways to address the challenges and attracted the attention of a large number of researchers [4–7].

Targeting is one of the properties that DDSs should possess. Magnetic targeting nanocarriers have been extensively investigated due to their easily adjustable and precise targeting properties [8,9]. Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) are the most commonly used magnetic material. However, the poor magnetic properties of Fe3O4 NPs in small sizes and under physiological conditions are a pressing issue. In addition, iron atoms in Fe3O4 might interact with hemoglobin, leading to side effects [10]. Spinel-type cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) with good chemical stability could avoid side effects and has excellent magnetic properties compared to Fe3O4 NPs [11], thus providing better tissue permeability and hemocompatibility [12]. In addition to magnetic targeting, CoFe2O4 also possesses microwave (MW) thermal conversion properties, endowing drug carriers with MW stimulated release properties [13]. Of the externally triggered drug release methods, MW radiation was a promising one with a penetration depth of 10–15 cm and a higher heating efficiency [14].

Positively charged NPs are easily absorbed by cell membranes, showing a fine targeting effect, while they are also easily cleared by macrophages before reaching the tumor site. Thus, intelligent DDSs, being negatively charged in the blood and becoming positively charged in tumor surroundings, could possess both targeting effect and long circulation time. This could be realized by pH-sensitive imine bonds and suitable capping agents. It was well known that the pH of normal tissues is 7.3–7.4, the extracellular pH of solid tumor sites is 6.2–6.9, and the pH of lysosomes is even lower (pH=5.0–6.0) [15,16]. Imine bonds are stable in the normal tissue environment and unstable in the tumor environment. Capping agents could be inorganic nanoparticles, peptides, proteins, carbohydrates, synthetic polymers, supramolecular assemblies, organic molecules, and DNA/RNA. Pursuiting capping agents with low or no potential risks and constructing capping systems sensitive to external stimuli are still cutting-edge topics in DDSs research [17]. Currently, the use of carbon dots (CDs) as a capping agent for mSiO2 has been reported by a few groups. CDs are fluorescent NPs with excellent biocompatibility and ease of functionalization and have been widely used in bioimaging [18]. CDs are negatively charged and possess fine water solubility due to functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups on their surface. Thus, CDs as capping agents should endow DDSs with not only excellent water dispersity but also negatively charged surface.

The morphology of the carriers is also crucial. Spheres with core@shell structure are the most common form of drug carriers. For multi-component functional materials, the function of the cores such as magnetism should be weakened due to the outer shells. Janus NPs, due to their anisotropic structure, tunable composition, and unique properties, are more likely to combine multiple functions, making them fascinating drug carriers. They often possess synergistic properties in addition to the properties of each component and have been extensively studied in biomedical field applications such as targeting[19], imaging [20], and drug delivery [21,22].

Here, we designed and synthesized a multifunctional magnetic Janus nanoplatform. “Ball-rod” shaped CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs magnetic Janus nanoparticles (MJNPs) were first prepared. The ball parts were spinel-type CoFe2O4, providing characteristics of magnetic targeting and MW responsiveness. Rod parts were mSiO2 with capping agent CDs and the linkage between them were pH sensitive imine bonds. CDs rich in functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups provided the carriers with good dispersibility and negatively charged surfaces, prolonging their circulation time. When carriers reached the tumor sites, the breaking of the pH sensitive imine bonds led to the detachment of CDs. The carriers became positively charged and easily absorbed by the cell membrane, prolonging residence time in the tumor site to enter into cells. Continuous release of DOX had a high killing effect on cancer cells. This platform integrated pH/MW response, fluorescence imaging, charge inversion, and smart release of the drug, should have a broad application prospect. The specific preparation and DOX release process was shown in Fig. 1.

Ferric chloride hexahydrate (99.0 %, FeCl3·6 H2O), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (99.0 %, CoCl2·6 H2O), sodium acetate (99.0 %, CH3COONa), ethylene glycol (99.5 %, EG), diethylene glycol (98 %, DEG), acetone (99.5 %, CH3COCH3), were purchased from China of Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ammonium hydroxide solution (25−28 %, NH3·H2O), tetraethyl orthosilicate (98 %, TEOS), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (98.0 %, CTAB), doxorubicin hydrochloride (98.0 %, DOX), methanol (99.5 %, CH3OH), dimethyl sulfoxide (99.7 %, DMSO), and absolute ethanol (99.7 %, CH3CH2OH) were provided by Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd (China). Ethylenediamine (99 %, EDA) and sodium salicylate (99.5 %, SS) were provided by Inno Chem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Glutaraldehyde (25 % in water, GA) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (98 %, APTES) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (St. Louis, MO, USA). All of the chemicals were used without any further purification. Double-distilled water used in all synthesis and purification processes was prepared by SZ-93 automatic double distiller (China Yarong).

CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method [23]. Firstly, 0.3202 g of SS and 135.1 μL of EDA were dispersed ultrasonically into 20 mL of ultrapure water. It was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL) and reacted at 180 ℃ for 3 h. The mixture was cooled naturally to room temperature to give a yellow-green solution, which was purified by silica-gel column chromatography using ethyl acetate as eluent. Rotary evaporation and freeze drying gave yellow-green powdery CDs.

In a typical procedure [13,24], 1.081 g (4 mmol) FeCl3·6 H2O and 0.476 g (2 mmol) CoCl2·6 H2O were dispersed in 30 mL mixed solvent (VEG: VDEG=1:5) under stirring at room temperature. Subsequently, 1.230 g (15 mmol) NaAc was added to the above dispersion with vigorous stirring for 30 min. It was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 200 ℃ for 10 h, and then the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The black solid obtained after magnetic separation was washed three times with anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water. Freeze-drying gave CoFe2O4.

CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB NPs were synthesized using the previously reported method [25,26]. Ultrasonically dispersing 17.5 mg of CoFe2O4 in 2.5 mL of ultrapure water gave a dispersion. Then, 17.5 mL of aqueous solution with CTAB (105 mg) was added to the above dispersion. After sonication for 15 min, the dispersion was transferred to a water bath at 40 ℃ with mechanical stirring. NH3·H2O (0.78 mL) and a certain amount of TEOS were added to the dispersion. The reaction proceeded for 30 min, and the solid obtained after magnetic separation was washed with ultrapure water and ethanol three times in sequence to obtain ball-rod shaped CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB.

Firstly, the above CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB NPs were dispersed in methanol (3.5 mL). APTES (0.5 mL) was then added and the mixture was stirred for 24 h at 25 ℃. After centrifugation, the NPs were washed well with methanol. After vacuum drying at 50 ℃, the solid was obtained as CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB-NH2. To remove CTAB, the above solid was dispersed into 50 mL of acetone and refluxed at 70 ℃ for 48 h. Old acetone was replaced by fresh one and refluxtion was continued for another 48 h. It was washed three times sequentially with ethanol and ultrapure water. After magnetic separation, the solid was CoFe2O4&mSiO2-NH2 [27].

CoFe2O4&mSiO2-NH2 NPs were further modified to obtain aldehyde-functionalized CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO. The above-synthesized sample CoFe2O4&mSiO2-NH2 NPs were dispersed in a solution of 0.2 mL GA and 3.8 mL ultrapure water. After vigorous stirring at 25 ℃ for 4 h, the product was recovered by magnetic separation and washed well with ultrapure water [28,29].

Next, the grafting of CDs was carried out. CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO NPs were dispersed in a 20 mL round bottom flask containing 4 mL of ultrapure water. 10 mg of CDs were dispersed in 1 mL of ultrapure water and transferred to the above suspension. The suspension was vigorously stirred at 25 ℃ for 4 h. The solid was separated magnetically and washed three times with ultrapure water. Freeze-drying gave CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs.

The resulting crystalline phases were characterized using D8 ADVANCE x-ray diffraction (Bruker, Germany). The Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements of the NPs were obtained using a Malvern Nano ZS particle size and Zeta potential analyzer (Kruss, UK). A H-7650 electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan) was utilized to record scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive x-ray (EDX) spectroscopy. The structure and morphology of the samples were observed using a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (Hitachi, Japan). EDX elemental mappings of the sample were examined on a JEM-2100Plus TEM (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray detector (Oxford Ultim Extreme). The properties of the magnetic materials were tested and characterized using a 7404 vibrating sample magnetometer (Lakeshore, USA). The absorbances of the solutions were measured by a TU-1900 ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectrophotometer (Purkinje, China). The cellular uptake was observed using a Ts2-FL fluorescence inverted microscope (Nikon, Japan). Using a Quantachrome AsiQ-C fully automated physical/chemical adsorption analyzer (USA), N2 adsorption/desorption techniques were employed to record the specific surface area and pore size distribution. Thermal conversion and drug release properties under MW stimulation were collected on a WB-3100 MW therapeutic instrument (Baoxing, China). Testo 835-H1 infrared thermometer (Testo AG, Germany) was used to measure the sample temperature. A Nicolet 5700 infrared spectrometer (Thermo, USA) was applied to measure the Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of the samples. The fluorescence properties of CDs (water as solvent) were measured using a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The weight loss of the samples in the range of 25–800 ℃ was tested using SDT Q600 DSC/DTA-TGA simultaneous thermal analyzers (USA) at 10 ℃ min−1 in an N2 environment. The pH was measured using a PH600L pH meter (Rex, China).

The stability of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs was investigated using the previously reported method [30]. A certain amount of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs was dispersed in PBS (1 mg mL−1) and then the mixture was placed in a thermostatic shaker for (37 ℃, 100 rpm) a certain time. Samples were taken out at different times (1 d, 2 d, 3 d, 4 d) and their morphologies were observed by SEM.

Drug loading content (DLC) and drug loading efficiency (DLE) were obtained using the following formulas:
(1)
DLC =
m
D O X
−
m
u n l o a d e d
m
t o t a l
× 100 %
(2)
DLE =
m
D O X
−
m
u n l o a d e d
m
D O X
× 100 %

The drug release behaviors were investigated via dialysis. DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (4 mg) NPs were scattered in 2 mL PBS (pH=7.4, 6.2, 5.3) and removed to dialysis membranes (MWCO, 1 kDa). The dialysis membrane was then put into the same PBS (20 mL) in a test tube, which was put on a constant temperature shaker (180 rpm). At different time points, the solution outside the dialysis membrane (4 mL) was removed and replaced with an equivalent new solution. The absorbance was measured by UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 485 nm. The cumulative drug release percentage (Er) was based on the following methodology:
(3)
Er =
V e
∑ 1
n - 1
c i
+
V o
c n
m drug
× 100 %

V e: volume of exchanged solution (4.0 mL), c i: concentration of DOX in the ith removed PBS, V o: total volume of original solution (20 mL), c n: DOX concentration of the residual PBS, m drug: total weight of DOX loaded in NPs (µg).
The release results of all drugs were measured three times. The drug release under MW irradiation (Frequency, 2.45 GHz) was investigated by irradiating samples for 5 minutes after sampling.
The NPs were dispersed in 10 mL PBS (pH=7.4) to obtain a solution with concentrations of 0.1 mg mL−1. The Zeta potential was determined by a Zetasizer NANO ZS potential particle size analyzer [31].

To determine the Zeta potential of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs in weakly acidic environments at different times, the specific treatment of the sample was as follows: DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (1 mg) NPs were dispersed in 10 mL PBS (pH=7.4, 6.2), and then the mixture oscillated at 180 rpm at 37 ℃ for a predetermined time. After magnetic separation, PBS was discarded and an equal volume of fresh PBS was added. The Zeta potential of the samples was measured immediately after uniform dispersion.

To analyze the cellular uptake of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs, MCF-7 (Human breast cancer cell, Gaining, China) cells were inoculated into 12-well plates (1×105 cells per well) and cultured for 24 h. After that, the medium (1 %, Penicillin-Streptomycin. 10 %, fetal bovine serum; Other parts, Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium. Procell Life, China) containing DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs were incubated with the cells for 2, 4, and 12 h. After the addition of 20 µL DAPI staining solution (ready-to-use, Adamas Life, China) per well, the 12-well plate was transferred to a 37 ℃ incubator for 8 min for staining. After aspirating the culture solution, the stained cells were rinsed three times with PBS and then visualized with a fluorescence-inverted microscope.

MCF-7 cells (5×104 cells per well) were seeded in 6-well plates and incubated in the incubator [32]. After the bottom of the plate was overgrown, the old medium was replaced with fresh medium containing nanocomposites (40 μg mL−1), and the cells were continued to culture for 4 h. The treated cells were then washed 3 times with pre-chilled PBS to remove the remaining nanocomposites. The labeled cells were digested with trypsin solution and transferred to a centrifuge tube. After centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 5 min, the cells were resuspended with PBS (pH=7.4) and collected in a flow cytometry tube. Flow cytometry (Beckman Coulter, USA) was used to detect the fluorescence intensity of cells. Untreated MCF-7 cells were used as the control group.

The cytotoxicity of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs and DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs MJNPs were evaluated using MTT (Mtt Formazan, Adamas life, China) test on A549 (human lung cancer cell, Procell Life, China), Hela (human cervical carcinoma cell, Gaining, China), and MCF-7. The cells were inoculated into 96-well plates (1×104 cells per well, 100 μL medium). After 12 h, the medium was substituted by the same volume of medium containing different concentrations of NPs, followed by an extra incubation for 24 h. The cells were incubated for 4 h after adding MTT (20 μL, 5.0 mg mL−1). Aspirating of medium containing MTT, DMSO (120 µL per well) was added and shaken for 10 min to fully dissolve the formazan crystals. The absorbance of the formazan solution was gauged by a microplate reader (Bio-Rad, USA) at 490 nm. The cell viability (%) was calculated as follows:
(4)
Cell
viability
( % ) =
I
d r u g
−
I
b l a n k
I
C o n t r o l
−
I
b l a n k
× 100 %

The procedure for studying cell viability under MW irradiation was similar to the above description, except that the cells were irradiated with MW (power: 20 W) for 5 min every 2 h after the addition of the DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs.

The CDs were obtained by hydrothermal reaction of SS and EDA ( Fig. 2a). The TEM image (Fig. 2b) showed that the CDs were uniformly distributed with a mean diameter of 3.6 nm. DLS result in Fig. 2c showed that the average diameter was about 4 nm, which was slightly larger than that obtained from TEM. The broad peak near 22.5° in the XRD pattern (Fig. 2d) belonged to the amorphous structure of the CDs. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2e), the dense peaks around 1500 cm−1 were typical benzene characteristic peaks. The CO (1627 cm−1), N−H/O−H (3123 cm−1, 3419 cm−1), and C−O (1143 cm−1) absorption peaks confirmed that the surface was enriched in −NH2 and −COOH groups, providing conditions for their attachment to the carriers’ surface. As seen in Fig. 2f, the optimal excitation wavelength of the synthesized CDs was 420 nm, and it exhibited strong green fluorescence at 520 nm. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was stable with little variation in PBS at pH=5.0−10.0 (Fig. 2g). UV-Vis spectrum showed that CDs had no absorption near 485 nm, indicating that CDs did not affect the subsequent measurement of DOX release (Fig. 2h).

CoFe2O4 NPs were prepared by a modified solvothermal method. The phase structure of CoFe2O4 was analyzed by XRD ( Fig. 3a). The diffraction peaks were consistent with the standard data (JCPDS, No. 22–1086) and no other peak appeared, indicating the crystal was a typical cubic spinel structure with high purity. The sharp and intense diffraction peaks indicated that the product had a high degree of crystallinity. In the FT-IR spectrum of CoFe2O4 NPs (Fig. 3b), the adsorption peak at 575 cm−1 was a stretching vibration characteristic peak of metal-oxygen (Co−O and Fe−O). SEM ( Fig. 4a) and TEM (Fig. 4b) showed that CoFe2O4 NPs were overall ball-shaped with rough surfaces. The mean size was around 190 nm (inset of Fig. 4b). The EDX curve (Fig. 3c) indicated that the synthesized NPs contained a certain amount of Co, Fe, and O atoms. The above proved that CoFe2O4 NPs were successfully synthesized.

CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB NPs were fabricated by interfacial energy-mediated anisotropic growth method. SiO2 rods grew on one side of CoFe2O4 NPs to form CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB using CoFe2O4 as seed, CTAB as template, TEOS as silicon source, and water as solvent. The preparation was carried out by fixing the amount of CoFe2O4 at 17.5 mg and varying the amount of TEOS. when the amounts of TEOS were 90 μL, 105 μL, and 120 μL, the length of the rod parts were about 200 nm (Fig. 4c), 350 nm (Fig. 4d), and 500 nm (Fig. 4e), respectively. The SiO2 rods assumed the function of drug-carrying. Longer rods meant higher drug-carrying capacity [33], while simultaneously meant a longer drug release cycle. After comprehensive consideration, the addition of 105 μL TEOS to obtain 350 nm rods was selected for our subsequent research. The ball-rod shaped Janus NPs were also displayed in the TEM image (Fig. 4f). In the XRD pattern (Fig. 3a), a broad peak appeared near 23°, indicating the amorphous structure of mSiO2. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3b), the absorption peak at 1076 cm−1 was a typical stretching vibration peak of the Si−O−Si bond [34]. The Zeta potential of CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB (-13.4 ± 1.9 mV) was lower than that of CoFe2O4 (-9.3 ± 2.5 mV), owing to the Si−OH groups on the surface of SiO2 rods (Fig. 3d). To avoid interference, CoFe2O4&mSiO2 was obtain by removing CTAB and the EDX and TGA were carried out. The EDX curve (Fig. 3c) showed that there was a large amount of Si element in addition to O, Co, and Fe elements. The TG curve (Fig. 3e) demonstrated that the weight loss of CoFe2O4&mSiO2 was 15.1 %, which was mainly due to the loss of water and −OH groups.

Surface amination of CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB and removal of CTAB gave CoFe2O4&mSiO2-NH2. The FT-IR (Fig. 3b) showed N−H stretching vibrational peaks at 3140 cm−1 and 3420 cm−1. A certain amount of N also appeared in the EDX elemental composition analysis (Fig. 3c). The Zeta potential was 15.6 ± 1.7 mV due to −NH2 in the surface of mSiO2. The total weight loss was 3.3 % higher than CoFe2O4&mSiO2, which was due to the loss of the organic long-chain structure of APTES (Fig. 3e).

The CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO NPs were obtained by a reaction of CoFe2O4&mSiO2-NH2 and GA, which was a homobifunctional reagent [35]. An imine bond was formed between the −NH2 group on the surface of CoFe2O4&mSiO2-NH2 and the −CHO group at one end in GA (Fig. 3f-①). In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3b), the characteristic vibration of CN appeared at 1643 cm−1 [36]. The H−CO absorption peak appeared near 1720 cm−1 [37], which contributed to the unreacted −CHO group at the other end of GA (Fig. 3b). The Zeta potential decreased to 10.2 ± 1.7 mV due to the −CHO on the surface. The weight loss in TG analysis was further increased (2.3 %), which was attributed to GA. The results demonstrated that the CoFe2O4&mSiO2-NH2 surfaces were successfully modified by −CHO groups.

Finally, CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs were achieved by the reaction between −NH2 groups on the CDs and −CHO groups on the CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO (Fig. 3f-②). The XRD diffraction (Fig. 3a) peaks of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs were the same as CoFe2O4&mSiO2, but the intensity decreased, indicating that the grafting of CDs did not change the crystal structures of CoFe2O4 and SiO2. SEM displayed that the mSiO2 surface of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (inset of Fig. 4d) was rougher than that of CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO (inset of Fig. 4g), which corresponded to the results of TEM (Fig. 4h and i). Fig. S1 (a-h) indicated that the obtained sample was a Janus structure with a length of 375 nm. Fig. S1 (b-f) showed the locations of the Fe, Co, Si, O, and C elements. The Fe and Co signals were located in the spherical part of the sample, the Si signal was in the rod part, and the O signal was in the spherical and rod part. The C elements were mainly distributed in the rod part, as demonstrated by the STEM-EDX line analysis in Fig. S1h. The merged mapping of Fig. S1g further confirmed the Janus structure of the final product.

Compared with the FT-IR spectrum of CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO, the intensity of the CN absorption peak at 1643 cm−1 was significantly enhanced for CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs, which was due to the formation of imine bonds between the CDs and CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO (Fig. 3b). The Zeta potential (Fig. 3d) became negative (-23.4 ± 1.8 mV) compared to positively charged CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO (10.2 ± 1.7 mV) due to the grafting of CDs. The weight loss of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs was 23.9 %, higher than that of CoFe2O4&mSiO2-CHO (20.5 %) (Fig. 3e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and responsiveness of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs MJNPs

ce:section.ce:para: The stability experiment (Fig. S3(a)-(d)) was performed. When CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs NPs were shaken on the shaker for 4 d, the morphology did not change significantly compared with the newly prepared NPs (Fig. 4g), and the Janus structure remained intact, indicating that the particles had good stability.

The magnetic properties of CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs at 310 K were studied ( Fig. 5a and b). The saturation magnetization (Ms), remanent magnetization (Mr), and coercivity (Hc) of CoFe2O4 were 83.2 emu g−1, 29.1 emu g−1 and 687.5 Oe, respectively. In an external magnetic field, the dispersion of CoFe2O4 rapidly changed from turbidity to clarification within 10 s (inset of Fig. 5a). The Ms, Mr and Hc of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs were 26.9 emu g−1, 2.3 emu g−1 and 90.2 Oe, respectively. The decrease in Ms value was due to the decrease in magnetic material content. The dispersion of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs became clear within 75 s (inset of Fig. 5a), also possessing a strong magnetic separation ability. The lower Mr meant that CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs might have good dispersibility in body fluids and blood, while the lower Hc meant that the NPs possessed higher magnetic loss during MW absorption [38].

The MW thermal transition properties were analyzed. The MW heat conversion curves (Fig. S2) of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (125 μg mL−1) at different powers (10 W, 20 W, 40 W, and 60 W) showed that higher power resulted in greater temperature change. After irradiation for 300 s, the temperature rose from 37.0 ℃ to 41.9 ℃, 43.7 ℃, 48.4 ℃, and 56.4 ℃, respectively. The temperature between 42 ℃ and 47 ℃ was more suitable since it could induce cancer cell death but did not harm normal cells [39]. 20 W resulting in a temperature rise to 43.7 ℃ was chosen as the subsequent experimental condition.

The MW thermal conversion heating curves of PBS, CoFe2O4, and CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs followed the same trend (Fig. 5c). The temperature of the suspensions increased with irradiation time. The temperature increased from 16.8 ℃ to 19.7 ± 0.3 ℃, 23.7 ± 0.5 ℃ and 25.5 ± 0.7 ℃, respectively, after 300 s of irradiation. The MW thermal transition capacity of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs was slightly lower than that of CoFe2O4 due to the decrease in absorption material content. However, it was still much higher than that of the PBS solution (Fig. 5c) and should be drug carriers with MW-triggered release behavior. The CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs MJNPs showed concentration dependent MW thermal transition property (Fig. 5d) and their MW thermal transition capacity increased with concentration. In the range of 0.0−500 µg mL−1, the temperature of the suspension increased by 2.9−11.8 ℃ after 300 s of MW irradiation.

The time-dependent Zeta potential of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs at constant pH was studied (Fig. 5e). At physiological pH (pH=7.4), the Zeta potential was −19.1 ± 2.3 mV at first and the variation within 160 min was negligible. In a weakly acidic environment (pH=6.2), the initial Zeta potential was −10.9 mV. It increased sharply at first and became positive (1.1 ± 0.7 mV) after 20 min. Afterward, the upward trend gradually slowed down. The capping agent CDs played a sealing role and prevented leakage of the drug. The negatively charged DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs should possess a long circulation time since they could escape the clearance by macrophage. The imine bonds broken in the extracellular microenvironment (pH < 6.2) with time and the exposure of −NH2 on mSiO2 happened, making the NPs positively charged. The positively charged NPs were more easily adsorbed by cells [40] and had a longer time to remain at the tumor site and release the drug.

The CTAB in CoFe2O4&SiO2-CTAB was removed by dissolution and refluxion in acetone to give CoFe2O4&mSiO2. The drug was loaded into CoFe2O4&mSiO2 and DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2 NPs were obtained. Then, the surface of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2 was aminated, treated with aldehyde, and grafted with CDs. Finally, DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs NPs were formed. The N2 adsorption/desorption curves of both CoFe2O4&mSiO2 and DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2 were typical II curves and H4 hysteresis loops as shown in Fig. 5f. The BET special surface area and pore sizes of CoFe2O4&mSiO2 were 183.6 m2 g−1 and 3.8 nm, while those of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2 were 78.8 m2 g−1, and 3.08 nm, respectively. The results indicated that DOX was successfully loaded into the pores of mSiO2 and occupied a certain amount of space. The TG loss (Fig. 3e) of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2 (21.0 %) was significantly greater than that of CoFe2O4&mSiO2 (15.1 %), which further indicated the successful drug loading. Moreover, the TG loss of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (27.3 %) was greater than CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (23.9 %), implying that most DOX remained in the NPs after grafting. DLC and DLE were calculated as 12.83 % and 25.81 %, respectively, according to Eqs. (1) and (2).

The drug release behaviors at different pH at 37 ℃ were studied ( Fig. 6a). Under neutral conditions, there was no drug release, indicating that the CDs acted as pores blockers. At pH=6.2, some of the imine bonds were broken, the CDs were detached from the carriers, and the drug was released. At 48 h, the cumulative release percentage reached 32.3 ± 2.4 %. At pH=5.3, the cumulative release percentage reached 53.2 ± 3.9 %. The cumulative release percentage increased with the decrease in pH. The result was mainly attributed to the pH sensitivity of imine bonds. In addition, due to the competition between H+ and positively charged DOX for binding sites, an increase in H+ concentration facilitated drug release.

At pH=6.2, the cumulative release percentage increased with increasing temperature (Fig. 6b). It might be due to the intensification of molecular thermal motion, which accelerated the cleavage of imine bonds and the entry of H+ into the mesopores.

In Fig. 6c, the cumulative release percentages with MW irradiation were much higher than those without irradiation under the same conditions, indicating that MW irradiation could promote drug release. When carriers were subjected to MW irradiation, MW energy was absorbed and converted into thermal energy. This thermal effect resulted in an increase in the temperature of the carriers, while synergistically acting with H+ to promote the diffusion and release of the drug. After 48 h, the cumulative release percentages were 42.4 ± 3.0 % and 72.9 ± 2.0 % at pH=6.2 and 5.3, respectively.

Three models [41–43] (Ritger-Peppas model, first-order kinetic model, and Higuchi kinetic model) were used to kinetically fit the in vitro drug release data (T = 37 ℃, MW, pH = 6.2, 5.3). By comparing the fitting results in Table S1, the correlation coefficient of the first-order kinetic model was the highest (R2 > 0.98), indicating that the release behavior was more in line with the first-order kinetic model. The “K” at pH = 5.3 was significantly higher than that of at pH = 6.2, showing that DOX was released more quickly at pH = 5.3. The “n” of the diffusion index in the Ritger-Peppas model were all lower than 0.45 (pH 6.2, 0.42; pH 5.3, 0.43), implying that the release mechanism of DOX was Fick diffusion. DOX was released from carriers in a diffusion manner.

From Fig. 7, weak green and red fluorescence inside the cells after 2 h of co-incubation with DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs were observed, indicating that a little bit of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs entered into the cells and relatively a little CDs and DOX were released. When co-incubation for 4 h, the intensity of green and red fluorescence increased, indicating that more CDs and DOX were present in the cells. After 8 h, the intensity of red fluorescence was further enhanced, indicating that a large number of carriers entered into cells and released DOX. The above indicated that DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs NPs were successfully uptake by cancer cells. The cellular uptake of nanocarriers under MW irradiation were also investigated by co-incubation with MCF-7 cells for 12 h (The method of MW irradiation was 300 s at 2 h intervals.). The red fluorescence intensity was significantly stronger than that without MW irradiation, suggesting that MW irradiation promoted drug release.

Core@shell NPs (CoFe2O4@mSiO2/CDs) with the same composition with CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs were prepared as a comparison and the details were in the supporting information (the specific characterization of CoFe2O4@mSiO2/CDs with the core@shell structure were shown in Fig. S4). Flow cytometry images (Fig. S5) showed that the uptake rate of Janus CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (48.5 %) was slightly greater than that of core@shell CoFe2O4@mSiO2/CDs (41.9 %), confirming that the Janus carries prepared were more conducive to cellular uptake.

To assess the cytotoxicity of the NPs, MTT assays were performed in different cancer cell lines including Hela, A549, and MCF-7 using different concentrations of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs (0−400 μg mL−1). Fig. 8a showed that the survival of the tumor cell lines decreased slightly with increasing concentrations of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs. When the concentration reached 400 μg mL−1, the cell survival rates were still higher than 90 %, indicating that the cytotoxicity of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs NPs were negligible and they were non-toxic as nanocarriers.

The cytotoxicity of free DOX and DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs on cancer cells were evaluated and the histograms of cell viability to DOX concentration were plotted. (Fig. 8b, c, d). The cytotoxicity of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs was higher than that of CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs at the same condition (Fig. 8a), implying that DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs released DOX was pharmacologically active. The cytotoxicity of free DOX and DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs increased with concentration. DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs displayed lower cytotoxicity than free DOX. This was due to the slow-release effect of the nanocarriers. The inhibition effect of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs on cancer cells under MW irradiation was stronger than that without MW irradiation, indicating that MW irradiation promoted the release of DOX. When the concentration of DOX reached 16 μg mL−1, the cell survival percents of Hela, A549, and MCF-7 under MW irradiation were 18.1 ± 2.4 %, 15.5 ± 3.4 %, and 13.4 ± 2.8 %, respectively. Thus, the results suggested that CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs MJNPs were very effective carriers for drug delivery.

In previously reported analogous studies, the toxicities of the carriers were slightly higher, and so were the NPs we prepared (Table S2). Their higher drug release content of DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs make them promising drug carriers.

In this paper, “ball-rod” shaped CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs MJNPs with CoFe2O4 balls and mSiO2 rods, which were coated with CDs via imine bonds, were prepared. The DDSs showed fine magnetic properties, MW thermal transition properties and pH responsiveness. DOX was loaded in the DDSs and the DLC was 12.83 %. The drug release behavior showed pH/MW dual responsiveness. CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs NPs were nontoxic. DOX-CoFe2O4&mSiO2/CDs exhibited a good inhibitory effect on tumor cell growth and the effect with MW irradiation was stronger than that without MW irradiation.

Guolin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Qiuhua Wu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yi Teng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Yuhai He: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that no competing interests exist. No writing assistance was utilized in the production of this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jinzhou Science and Technology Program (JZ2023B068) and the Transverse Project of Jinzhou Medical University (H2023033/H2023039).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134903.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional “ball-rod” Janus nanoparticles boosting Fenton reaction for ferroptosis therapy of non-small cell lung cancer

Ma, Multifunctional “ball-rod” Janus nanoparticles boosting Fenton reaction for ferroptosis therapy of non-small cell lung cancer, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 621 (2022) 12-23.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering particles for sensing applications via in-situ synthesizing carbon dots@SiO2 photonic crystals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yu, X. Zhang, T. Qiu, H. Liu, J. Guo, Q. Li, Z. Zhang, S. Chen, Engineering particles for sensing applications via in-situ synthesizing carbon dots@SiO2 photonic crystals, Chem. Eng. J. 465 (2023) 142851.
